2605.12411.title: Predicting Decisions of AI Agents from Limited Interaction through Text-Tabular Modeling
2605.12411.paper_summary: AI agents negotiate and transact in natural language with unfamiliar counterparts: a buyer bot facing an unknown seller, or a procurement assistant negotiating with a supplier. In such interactions, the counterpart's LLM, prompts, control logic, and rule-based fallbacks are hidden, while each decision can have monetary consequences. We ask whether an agent can predict an unfamiliar counterpart's next decision from a few interactions. To avoid real-world logging confounds, we study this problem in controlled bargaining and negotiation games, formulating it as target-adaptive text-tabular prediction: each decision point is a table row combining structured game state, offer history, and dialogue, while $K$ previous games of the same target agent, i.e., the counterpart being modeled, are provided in the prompt as labeled adaptation examples. Our model is built on a tabular foundation model that represents rows using game-state features and LLM-based text representations, and adds LLM-as-Observer as an additional representation: a small frozen LLM reads the decision-time state and dialogue; its answer is discarded, and its hidden state becomes a decision-oriented feature, making the LLM an encoder rather than a direct few-shot predictor. Training on 13 frontier-LLM agents and testing on 91 held-out scaffolded agents, the full model outperforms direct LLM-as-Predictor prompting and game+text features baselines. Within this tabular model, Observer features contribute beyond the other feature schemes: at $K=16$, they improve response-prediction AUC by about 4 points across both tasks and reduce bargaining offer-prediction error by 14%. These results show that formulating counterpart prediction as a target-adaptive text-tabular task enables effective adaptation, and that hidden LLM representations expose decision-relevant signals that direct prompting does not surface.
2605.12411.paper_summary_zh: AI 代理與不熟悉的對手進行自然語言的談判和交易：一個買方機器人面對一個未知的賣方，或者一個採購助手與供應商進行談判。在這種互動中，對手的 LLM、提示、控制邏輯和基於規則的後備方案都是隱藏的，而每個決策都可能帶來金錢上的後果。我們詢問一個代理是否能從幾次互動中預測不熟悉對手的下一個決策。為了避免現實世界日誌的混淆，我們在受控的議價和談判遊戲中研究這個問題，將其表述為目標自適應文本-表格預測：每個決策點是一個表格行，結合了結構化的遊戲狀態、報價歷史和對話，而 $K$ 個同一目標代理的先前遊戲，即被建模的對手，作為標記的適應示例提供在提示中。我們的模型建立在一個表格基礎模型上，使用遊戲狀態特徵和基於 LLM 的文本表示來表示行，並添加 LLM-as-Observer 作為額外的表示：一個小型的凍結 LLM 讀取決策時的狀態和對話；它的回答被丟棄，其隱藏狀態成為一個以決策為導向的特徵，使 LLM 成為編碼器，而不是直接的少量預測器。在 13 個前沿 LLM 代理上進行訓練，並在 91 個保留的支架代理上進行測試，完整模型的表現超過了直接 LLM-as-Predictor 提示和遊戲+文本特徵基準。在這個表格模型中，Observer 特徵的貢獻超過了其他特徵方案：在 $K=16$ 時，它們在兩個任務中將響應預測 AUC 提高了約 4 點，並將議價報價預測誤差降低了 14%。這些結果顯示，將對手預測表述為目標自適應文本-表格任務能夠實現有效的適應，並且隱藏的 LLM 表示揭示了決策相關的信號，而直接提示並未顯示這些信號。
2605.12411.author: Eilam Shapira et.al.
2605.12411.authors: Eilam Shapira, Moshe Tennenholtz, Roi Reichart
